soap:
SOAP · lance-format/lance#7571 — fix: preserve structural page load errors

Subjective
  ddupg (core) fixes a bug where a structural page-load I/O error (e.g. an object-store range-read timeout) gets masked by a subsequent unwrap() panic in the primitive decoder, producing a misleading PanicException in Python instead of the real OSError. StructuralBatchDecodeStream bumped rows_scheduled before awaiting the UnloadedPageShard; if that page future errored, the stream still returned an emitted error item but remained pollable, so .buffered(batch_readahead) consumers could poll again, at which point the primitive decoder saw rows "scheduled" that were never accepted and hit front_mut().unwrap() on an empty VecDeque. Two-file diff (+132/-46), CI green (30/30), mergeable.

Objective (traced against code at 495afdc1 in a local worktree)
  - decoder.rs:1916-1929 — into_stream's unfold now matches next_batch_task().await; on Err it sets rows_remaining = 0 and emits a synthetic ReadBatchTask resolving to Err(err), still returning slf so the stream is polled once more; on that poll next_batch_task returns Ok(None) (rows_remaining==0) and terminates cleanly. One-shot, no re-entry. Confirmed the bug source at line 1844 (rows_scheduled set before the per-message await/accept_page loop).
  - primitive.rs:3663-3674 — front_mut().unwrap() replaced with a let-else returning Error::internal(...) including field name, data type, requested/remaining rows, rows_drained_in_current, and queued-page count. Error::internal is the correct existing variant.
  - Consumer chain verified: io/exec/scan.rs:410-421 flattens via try_flatten().try_buffered(batch_readahead). Read futures-util 0.3.32 TryBuffered::poll_next — outer stream yields Ok(task), inner future's Err surfaces as a forwarded item without short-circuiting the outer poll, so the outer stream must self-terminate — which this fix does.
  - Ran both new tests + full cargo test -p lance-encoding --lib: 397 passed, 0 failed, 5 ignored (pre-existing). fmt clean; clippy covered by CI's green run.
  - Not covered: sibling legacy BatchDecodeStream::into_stream (decoder.rs:1509-1535, is_structural==false, v2.0 non-structural files) has the identical pre-fix shape — next_task.transpose() turns Err into Some((task, slf)) without adjusting rows_remaining. Same bug class, older path, left untouched.

Assessment
  risk: med (confirmed from content) — error-handling/control-flow fix in the streaming decode path; no on-disk format, no concurrency primitives, no unsafe; well-scoped, reasoning checks out against actual futures semantics.
  urgency: low — no active incident, but resolves a confusing panic that surfaces in place of actionable I/O errors (timeouts), affecting production debuggability today.
  escalation: none · could not read: none (full diff, no omitted files).
  Test coverage genuine: both regression tests run and confirmed to hit the intended paths with assertions on specific error content.

Plan
  Structural-path fix is correct, narrowly scoped, well-tested, matches its stated root cause. Substantive gap: the twin bug in the legacy (is_structural==false) decode stream is unaddressed — worth a non-blocking follow-up.

RECOMMENDATION: approve
  confidence: high — root cause verified line-by-line, new tests + full lance-encoding lib suite run green, futures try_buffered semantics confirmed against crate source, upstream CI 30/30. What would make this wrong: a caller polling into_stream() outside the try_flatten/try_buffered pattern expecting more batches after an error — grep confirms decoder.rs is the only construction/consumption site.
summary: Fixes a real bug: structural page-load I/O errors (e.g. object-store timeouts) were masked by a later unwrap() panic in the primitive decoder because the decode stream stayed pollable after emitting an error. Root cause verified line-by-line (rows_scheduled bumped before the page future is awaited), fix verified against futures::try_buffered semantics, both new regression tests run and pass, full lance-encoding lib suite green (397 passed). One non-blocking gap: the identical bug pattern remains in the legacy (is_structural=false) BatchDecodeStream::into_stream, unaddressed by this PR.

comments.label: issue
comments.body: non-blocking: BatchDecodeStream::into_stream (the legacy/non-structural path, still live for v2.0 files via create_decode_stream at decoder.rs:2123 when is_structural is false) has the same pre-fix shape this PR corrects for the structural path: next_task.transpose() turns an Err from next_batch_task into Some((task, slf)) without touching rows_remaining, so the stream stays pollable and can resume against stale scheduler state after an error. Worth a follow-up applying the same rows_remaining = 0 + early-return pattern, or a comment noting why v2.0 files aren't exposed to the equivalent panic.

comments.label: question
comments.body: question: self.rows_scheduled = scan_line.scheduled_so_far is set before the for message in scan_line.decoders loop that awaits each page and calls accept_page. If a scan_line carries multiple messages and an error occurs partway through, rows_scheduled reflects pages never accepted for the other fields too. This fix (killing the whole stream via rows_remaining = 0) papers over this since the stream terminates right after — confirming: is a scan_line with multiple decoders entries and a mid-way failure actually possible, or does the scheduler always emit one page per scan line? If possible, worth a one-line comment on why partial acceptance is safe here.

comments.label: praise
comments.body: praise: Good replacement of the panic-prone unwrap() with a diagnostic-rich Error::internal — field name, data type, requested/remaining rows, and queue depth make any future recurrence of this invariant violation actionable instead of an opaque panic.

comments.label: praise
comments.body: praise: test_structural_decode_stream_stops_after_page_load_error verified — constructs a genuinely failing UnloadedPageShard and asserts both that the original error surfaces (not a panic) and that the stream terminates on the next poll. Ran it directly; passes and correctly exercises the .buffered(2) readahead scenario.
